parsed.justification: The cycle is ig_spread -> sp500_ret -> vix -> ig_spread. All three edges are bidirectional candidates. The causal question is which direction is most defensible for each edge. For sp500_ret -> vix: while it is true that large negative S&P 500 returns cause VIX to spike (leverage effect, demand for puts), the more fundamental causal structure is that VIX is a forward-looking options-implied measure that ANTICIPATES volatility rather than simply reacting to realized returns. VIX rises BEFORE or concurrent with equity selloffs as investors buy protective puts in anticipation of further declines. Reversing to vix -> sp500_ret captures the well-documented mechanism whereby elevated fear/uncertainty (high VIX) depresses equity returns going forward — the risk premium channel. This is the standard asset-pricing interpretation: higher implied volatility embeds a higher equity risk premium, which mechanically lowers current equity prices (negative contemporaneous return) as the required return rises. The other two edges are more clearly directional: ig_spread -> sp500_ret is well-supported because credit conditions causally affect equity valuations through tighter financing costs and recession signals; vix -> ig_spread is well-supported because broad risk aversion elevates credit spreads as investors demand compensation for corporate credit risk. Reversing sp500_ret -> vix to vix -> sp500_ret breaks the cycle while preserving the most causally defensible structure: VIX (fear/risk-aversion) causes equity market returns (through the risk premium channel), credit spreads widen with elevated VIX (risk aversion channel), and wider credit spreads pressure equity valuations (financing cost and recession-signal channel).
